title: Alectryon grandis
intro: Alectryon grandis, commonly known as the 'Ironwood', is a flowering plant species in the family Sapindaceae. The health benefits of Alectryon grandis include its potential to treat various ailments, such as fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues, due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of this plant include flavonoids, alkaloids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Alectryon grandis is traditionally used to make herbal preparations, including teas, infusions, and capsules, which are used to treat a range of health conditions, although further research is needed to confirm its efficacy.
taxonomy: Alectryon grandis is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Sapindaceae. This plant belongs to the genus Alectryon, which is native to the tropical regions of the Pacific Islands and Australia. The Sapindaceae family consists of approximately 1,200 species, many of which are commonly known as soapberry or soapnut trees. Alectryon grandis is also known as the ironwood or beach ironwood due to its hard wood. The classification of Alectryon grandis is further divided into the order Sapindales, which includes other plants like the lychee and the rambutan.
uses: Alectryon grandis is a plant with various traditional uses, including its application in treating fever and wounds. In some regions, the plant's extracts are used to alleviate pain and reduce inflammation. Traditionally, it is also used to treat skin conditions such as eczema and dermatitis. Additionally, the plant is believed to have antibacterial properties, which can help prevent infection. More research is needed to confirm the efficacy of Alectryon grandis in treating these conditions.

side_effects: Alectryon grandis, also known as the "pandanus tree", has been reported to have some potential side effects.
